sheets.title: S3 Storage Classes
sheets.summary: Picking the right S3 tier: access frequency, retrieval cost, minimum duration.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
S3 charges you in three dimensions: storage, requests, and retrieval. Pick the class that matches how often you actually read the data.

## When to use what

| Class | Min duration | Min object size billed | Retrieval | Fits |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | none | none | free | Hot data, websites, active analytics |
| Intelligent-Tiering | none | 128 KB monitored | free | Unknown / shifting access patterns |
| Standard-IA | 30 days | 128 KB | per-GB fee | Backups, DR copies accessed monthly |
| One Zone-IA | 30 days | 128 KB | per-GB fee | Re-creatable secondary copies (single AZ) |
| Glacier Instant Retrieval | 90 days | 128 KB | per-GB, ms | Archive you still need to query instantly |
| Glacier Flexible | 90 days | 40 KB | minutes to hours | Quarterly compliance reads |
| Glacier Deep Archive | 180 days | 40 KB | up to 12 hours | 7+ year retention, lowest cost |

## Key facts
- Intelligent-Tiering has no retrieval fees but charges a per-object monitoring fee — bad for millions of tiny files.
- Early-delete fees apply if you delete or transition before the minimum duration.
- One Zone-IA is ~20% cheaper than Standard-IA but loses an AZ outage.
- Lifecycle rules transition objects between classes; you cannot transition Glacier Deep Archive back without a restore.

## Pitfalls
- Putting tiny log files into IA — the 128 KB billing minimum makes it more expensive than Standard.
- Forgetting that Glacier restores are objects, not in-place tier flips.

## Related
S3 Lifecycle, S3 Storage Lens, S3 Inventory, Athena (queries Glacier IR directly).

sheets.title: EC2 Instance Families
sheets.summary: Letter prefix tells you the workload shape: M/T/A general, C compute, R/X/Z memory, I/D storage, P/G/Inf/Trn accelerated.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
The family letter encodes the resource ratio. Generation number (M7i, C7g) just means newer silicon.

## Families

| Family | Profile | Typical workload |
|---|---|---|
| M (M7i, M7g, M7a) | Balanced vCPU:RAM 1:4 | App servers, small DBs, generic dev |
| T (T3, T4g) | Burstable, CPU credits | Low-traffic sites, microservices, build agents |
| A (A1) | Arm Graviton, low-cost | Scale-out web tiers built for Arm |
| C (C7i, C7g, C7gn) | CPU-heavy 1:2 | Batch, HPC, encoders, ad serving |
| R (R7i, R7g) | RAM-heavy 1:8 | In-memory DBs, ElastiCache hosts, SAP |
| X (X2idn, X2iedn) | Extreme RAM 1:32 | SAP HANA, large in-memory analytics |
| Z (z1d) | High single-thread freq | EDA, gaming servers, simulation |
| I (I4i, Im4gn) | Local NVMe SSD | NoSQL, search, transactional logs |
| D (D3, D3en) | Dense HDD | Hadoop, MapReduce, log warehouses |
| H (H1) | Sequential HDD | Distributed file systems |
| P (P5) | NVIDIA GPUs | ML training, CUDA HPC |
| G (G5, G6) | NVIDIA GPUs | Inference, graphics, transcoding |
| Inf (Inf2) | AWS Inferentia | Cost-optimized inference |
| Trn (Trn1) | AWS Trainium | Large-model training |

## Pitfalls
- T-class CPU credits exhaust under sustained load; switch to T-unlimited or pick M.
- Graviton (g suffix) needs Arm-compatible binaries — not a free swap.
- Burstable instances have no SLA on baseline performance once credits are zero.

## Related
Compute Optimizer, Spot, Savings Plans, Nitro Enclaves.

sheets.title: EBS Volume Types
sheets.summary: gp3 is the default; io2 Block Express for sub-ms transactional; st1/sc1 for streaming HDD workloads.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Default to gp3. Reach for io2 only when you need >16k IOPS with durability SLA, st1/sc1 only for big sequential HDD.

## Comparison

| Volume | Max IOPS | Max throughput | Durability | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| gp3 SSD | 16,000 | 1,000 MB/s | 99.8–99.9% | New default — IOPS/throughput independent of size |
| gp2 SSD | 16,000 | 250 MB/s | 99.8–99.9% | Legacy, scales IOPS with size (3 IOPS/GB) |
| io2 SSD | 64,000 | 1,000 MB/s | 99.999% | High-perf databases, critical writes |
| io2 Block Express | 256,000 | 4,000 MB/s | 99.999% | SAP HANA, Oracle RAC, sub-ms latency |
| io1 SSD | 64,000 | 1,000 MB/s | 99.8–99.9% | Legacy provisioned IOPS |
| st1 HDD | 500 | 500 MB/s | 99.8–99.9% | Big data, log processing, throughput-bound |
| sc1 HDD | 250 | 250 MB/s | 99.8–99.9% | Cold archive that still needs a filesystem |

## Key facts
- gp3 baseline: 3,000 IOPS + 125 MB/s free, then pay per provisioned IOPS/throughput.
- io2 durability quoted as 99.999% (5 nines) — an order of magnitude better than gp3.
- st1/sc1 cannot boot — root volumes must be SSD.
- Snapshots are incremental and stored in S3 — billed only for changed blocks.

## Pitfalls
- Migrating gp2 to gp3 with the same IOPS is cheaper for most workloads — verify and move.
- Multi-attach is io1/io2 only and needs cluster-aware filesystems.

## Related
EBS Snapshots, AMI, FSx, Instance Store (ephemeral).

sheets.title: ALB vs NLB vs GWLB
sheets.summary: ALB at L7 for HTTP routing; NLB at L4 for TCP/UDP/static IP; GWLB for inline appliance chains.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
ALB inspects HTTP. NLB just forwards packets. GWLB hairpins traffic through third-party appliances.

## Comparison

| Feature | ALB | NLB | GWLB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer | 7 | 4 | 3/4 (GENEVE) |
| Protocols | HTTP, HTTPS, gRPC, WebSocket | TCP, UDP, TLS | IP |
| Target types | Instance, IP, Lambda | Instance, IP, ALB | Instance, IP |
| Static IP / EIP | No (DNS only) | Yes, one per AZ | Yes via endpoints |
| Source IP preservation | Via X-Forwarded-For | Yes (true client IP) | Yes (encapsulated) |
| TLS termination | Yes | Yes (passthrough or terminate) | No |
| mTLS | Yes (verify or passthrough) | Passthrough only | n/a |
| Use case | Microservices, host/path routing | Gaming, IoT, ultra-low latency | IDS/IPS, NGFW, DLP appliances |

## Key facts
- ALB supports redirect, fixed-response, weighted target groups, and authentication via Cognito/OIDC.
- NLB preserves the client source IP (SCT preservation) without proxy protocol when targeting instances.
- GWLB pairs with GWLB endpoints (GWLBe) and uses GENEVE on port 6081 to encapsulate flows.
- ALB and NLB cross-zone load balancing differ: NLB charges for cross-AZ data when enabled.

## Pitfalls
- Forgetting that NLB targets receive the real client IP — security groups must allow it, not the LB's.
- Routing WebSockets through CloudFront → ALB requires ALB; NLB will work but loses L7 features.

## Related
API Gateway, Global Accelerator, Route 53.

sheets.title: SQS vs SNS vs EventBridge
sheets.summary: SQS = queue (1-to-1, pull). SNS = pub/sub (fan-out, push). EventBridge = routing bus with schemas and rules.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
If one consumer pulls work: SQS. If many consumers each get a copy: SNS. If you want filtering, schemas, and SaaS sources: EventBridge.

## Comparison

| Feature | SQS | SNS | EventBridge |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Queue, pull | Topic, push | Bus, push with rules |
| Delivery | At-least-once (standard) or exactly-once (FIFO) | At-least-once | At-least-once |
| Ordering | FIFO queues only | FIFO topics only | No |
| Throughput | Standard: unlimited; FIFO: 3k msg/s (300 w/o batching) | Standard: high; FIFO: 300 TPS | 10k events/s/account default |
| Max msg size | 256 KB (S3 ext to 2 GB) | 256 KB | 256 KB |
| Retention | 1 min – 14 days | n/a (push) | 24 hr archive replay |
| DLQ | Native | Native (to SQS) | Native (to SQS) |
| Filtering | None | Message attribute filters | Rich JSON pattern matching |
| Schema registry | No | No | Yes |

## Key facts
- Classic fan-out: SNS topic → multiple SQS queues, each subscriber gets a copy.
- EventBridge has 100+ SaaS partner sources (Datadog, PagerDuty, Stripe).
- SQS visibility timeout default 30s; extend with ChangeMessageVisibility for long jobs.
- EventBridge Pipes connect a source straight to a target with optional filter + transform.

## Pitfalls
- Using SNS for ordered work — only FIFO topics preserve order, and only with SQS FIFO subscribers.
- Forgetting DLQ — silent failures will pile up.

## Related
Step Functions, Kinesis (for ordered streaming at scale), MSK.

sheets.title: Multi-AZ vs Multi-Region
sheets.summary: Multi-AZ for HA inside a region; Multi-Region for disaster recovery, geographic compliance, and lower RTO.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Multi-AZ survives an AZ outage with near-zero data loss and is cheap. Multi-Region survives a region outage but costs a lot more in data transfer and operational complexity.

## RTO / RPO targets

| Strategy | Typical RTO | Typical RPO | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backup & restore | hours | hours | $ |
| Pilot light (multi-region) | tens of minutes | minutes | $$ |
| Warm standby (multi-region) | minutes | seconds | $$$ |
| Active/active (multi-region) | seconds | near zero | $$$$ |
| Multi-AZ (single region) | seconds | seconds | $ |

## Services per scope
- **Multi-AZ built-in**: RDS Multi-AZ, ElastiCache, ALB/NLB, EFS, MSK, Aurora (cluster spans AZs by default).
- **Multi-Region built-in**: S3 CRR/MRAP, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, Route 53, Global Accelerator, KMS multi-region keys, IAM, CloudFront.
- **Manual replication needed**: EBS snapshots, AMIs, Lambda functions (or use CloudFormation StackSets).

## Key facts
- Aurora Global: <1s replication, sub-minute promotion of secondary region.
- DynamoDB Global Tables: last-writer-wins multi-active, eventually consistent across regions.
- Route 53 health checks + failover routing is the cheapest cross-region failover.

## Pitfalls
- Counting Multi-AZ as DR — a logical bug, ransomware, or bad deploy hits all AZs.
- Forgetting data egress costs between regions.

## Related
AWS Resilience Hub, AWS Backup, Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS).

sheets.title: Savings Plans vs Reserved Instances vs Spot
sheets.summary: Spot for fault-tolerant workloads (up to 90% off), Savings Plans for predictable spend, RIs for legacy RDS/ElastiCache lock-in.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
For EC2/Fargate/Lambda, Savings Plans have replaced Reserved Instances. RIs are still the only option for RDS, ElastiCache, OpenSearch, Redshift, DynamoDB.

## Options

| Model | Commitment | Discount | Flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spot | none | up to 90% | Can be reclaimed with 2-min warning |
| Compute Savings Plan | 1 or 3 yr, $/hr | up to 66% | Any region, family, size, OS, tenancy, EC2/Fargate/Lambda |
| EC2 Instance Savings Plan | 1 or 3 yr, $/hr | up to 72% | Locked to family + region; size/OS flexible |
| Standard RI (RDS et al) | 1 or 3 yr | up to 72% | Can change AZ; sellable on Marketplace (EC2 historical) |
| Convertible RI (EC2 historical) | 1 or 3 yr | up to 54% | Can swap family/OS/tenancy |
| On-Demand | none | 0 | Full flexibility |

## Key facts
- Payment options: All Upfront (best discount), Partial Upfront, No Upfront.
- Spot Fleet + Capacity Rebalancing handles interruption gracefully across pools.
- Savings Plans don't reserve capacity — pair with On-Demand Capacity Reservations if you need both.
- Compute Optimizer recommends Savings Plan and rightsizing actions.

## Pitfalls
- Committing 3-year All Upfront on a workload you'll re-platform in 6 months.
- Using Spot for stateful single-node workloads — interruption = data loss.

## Related
Cost Explorer, Budgets, Compute Optimizer, Trusted Advisor.

sheets.title: IAM Users, Roles, and Policies
sheets.summary: Users are long-lived identities; roles are assumable, short-term credentials; policies attach to either or to the resource.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
A principal (user, role, federated identity) gets effective permissions = intersection of identity policies, resource policies, SCPs, permission boundaries, and session policies. Explicit deny always wins.

## Building blocks

| Concept | Purpose |
|---|---|
| User | Long-term identity for a human or legacy app (avoid for workloads) |
| Group | Bag of users that share managed policies — has no credentials itself |
| Role | Identity assumed via STS; returns temporary credentials |
| Identity policy | Attached to user/group/role — "who can do what" |
| Resource policy | Attached to resource (S3 bucket, KMS key, SNS topic) — "who can touch me" |
| Trust policy | On a role — "who can AssumeRole me" |
| Permission boundary | Caps the max permissions a user/role can ever have |
| SCP | Org-wide guardrail — denies are absolute, allow is necessary but not sufficient |
| Session policy | Passed at AssumeRole time, further restricts the session |

## Key facts
- STS AssumeRole default session: 1 hour; max 12 hours (configurable).
- AssumeRoleWithSAML and AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity for federation (no IAM user needed).
- IAM Roles Anywhere lets workloads outside AWS get temp creds using X.509 certs.
- Use IAM Access Analyzer to find public/cross-account exposure and unused access.

## Pitfalls
- Long-lived access keys on EC2 — use an instance profile (role) instead.
- Forgetting that explicit `Deny` in an SCP, resource policy, or boundary wins regardless of allow.

## Related
IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO), Organizations, Cognito, Secrets Manager.

sheets.title: CloudWatch vs CloudTrail vs Config
sheets.summary: CloudWatch = metrics/logs/alarms. CloudTrail = who called what API. Config = what does the resource look like over time.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Three different lenses on your AWS estate. Most production setups need all three.

## Comparison

| Tool | Records | Granularity | Retention | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CloudWatch Metrics | Numeric time series | 1 sec (high-res) – 1 min | 15 months (rolled up) | Dashboards, alarms, autoscaling |
| CloudWatch Logs | Log lines | per-event | Configurable, indefinite | App/system logs, Lambda output |
| CloudWatch Alarms | Threshold breaches | 10s–min | event-driven | Page on-call, trigger Auto Scaling |
| CloudTrail | API calls | per-event | 90 days in event history; unlimited in S3 trail | Forensics, audit, who-deleted-what |
| Config | Resource configuration snapshots + change history | per-change | as long as you keep it | Compliance, drift, point-in-time view |

## Key facts
- CloudTrail captures management events by default. Data events (S3 GetObject, Lambda Invoke) cost extra.
- CloudTrail Lake stores events in SQL-queryable form for up to 7 years.
- Config Rules evaluate compliance (managed or custom Lambda); Conformance Packs bundle them.
- CloudWatch Logs Insights uses a purpose-built query language for ad-hoc log analysis.
- VPC Flow Logs land in CloudWatch Logs or S3 — they're network, not CloudTrail.

## Pitfalls
- Using CloudTrail to debug runtime errors — that's Logs.
- Forgetting that CloudTrail event history (90 days) is not a long-term audit trail; create an S3 trail.

## Related
EventBridge (route Config and CloudTrail events), Security Hub, GuardDuty, X-Ray.

sheets.title: VPC, Subnets, and Route Tables
sheets.summary: Subnets are AZ-scoped CIDR slices; their route table determines public/private/isolated; longest-prefix match wins.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
A subnet is public if its route table has a default route to an Internet Gateway. Private if it routes via a NAT. Isolated if it has no internet route at all.

## Routing

| Subnet type | Route 0.0.0.0/0 to | Outbound internet | Inbound from internet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public | IGW | Yes | Yes (if public IP / EIP) |
| Private | NAT Gateway | Yes | No |
| Isolated | (none) | No | No |

- **Longest-prefix match** wins: a /24 route beats a /16 route to the same destination.
- Local route to the VPC CIDR is implicit and unremovable.
- Each subnet has exactly one route table (the main RT if not explicitly associated).

## VPC endpoints
- **Gateway endpoints** (free): S3, DynamoDB. Added as a route in the RT.
- **Interface endpoints** (PrivateLink, hourly + GB): every other service. Creates an ENI in your subnet.
- **GWLB endpoints**: insert third-party appliances in the data path.

## Key facts
- AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet (.0 network, .1 router, .2 DNS, .3 future, .255 broadcast equiv).
- NAT Gateway is AZ-scoped — for HA put one per AZ and route each private subnet to its local NAT.
- Egress-only IGW is the IPv6 equivalent of a NAT.
- Transit Gateway centralizes VPC↔VPC and VPC↔on-prem peering at scale.

## Pitfalls
- Single NAT Gateway across AZs — cross-AZ data fees + an AZ outage breaks egress.
- Forgetting NACLs are stateless — open both directions.

## Related
Transit Gateway, Direct Connect, Site-to-Site VPN, Network Firewall.

sheets.title: RDS vs Aurora vs DynamoDB
sheets.summary: RDS for vanilla managed relational; Aurora for cloud-native relational scaling; DynamoDB for serverless key-value/document at any scale.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Use the simplest thing that fits. Vanilla Postgres or MySQL → RDS. Need read scale, global, or serverless v2 elasticity → Aurora. Predictable single-digit ms NoSQL at any scale → DynamoDB.

## Comparison

| Feature | RDS | Aurora | DynamoDB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model | Relational (Postgres, MySQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server) | Postgres- / MySQL-compatible cloud-native | Key-value + document |
| Storage | EBS volumes | Distributed 6-way replicated across 3 AZs | Managed, auto-sharded |
| Max size | 64 TiB (engine-dep.) | 128 TiB | Unlimited (per-item 400 KB) |
| Read scaling | Up to 15 read replicas | Up to 15 Aurora Replicas with sub-10ms lag | Auto, on-demand or provisioned |
| Multi-region | Manual (read replicas) | Aurora Global Database, <1s lag | Global Tables, multi-active |
| Failover | Multi-AZ standby, 60–120s | Replica promotion, <30s | n/a (transparent) |
| Pricing | Instance + storage | Instance + storage I/O (or I/O-optimized flat) + storage | Per-RCU/WCU or per-request, plus storage |
| Serverless | n/a | Aurora Serverless v2, ACU-based | Always serverless |

## Key facts
- DynamoDB has two consistency modes: eventually (default) and strongly (2x RCU).
- DAX adds an in-memory cache in front of DynamoDB for microsecond reads.
- Aurora Backtrack rewinds the cluster in place up to 72 hours (MySQL only).
- RDS supports Performance Insights and IAM database authentication.

## Pitfalls
- Picking DynamoDB without a clear primary key access pattern — scans get expensive fast.
- Aurora Serverless v2 doesn't scale to zero (only v1 did, and v1 is deprecated).

## Related
ElastiCache, OpenSearch, Redshift, DocumentDB, Neptune.

sheets.title: Lambda Limits and Patterns
sheets.summary: 15-min timeout, 10 GB memory, 1000 concurrent default. Use provisioned concurrency for cold-start-sensitive paths.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Lambda scales horizontally by spinning new execution environments. Cold starts hit when you scale up or after idle. Bound your blast radius with reserved concurrency.

## Hard limits

| Resource | Limit |
|---|---|
| Max execution time | 15 minutes |
| Memory | 128 MB – 10,240 MB (vCPU scales with memory) |
| Ephemeral /tmp | 512 MB – 10,240 MB |
| Deployment package | 50 MB zipped / 250 MB unzipped (250 MB for layers) |
| Container image | 10 GB |
| Account concurrency | 1,000 default (raisable) |
| Burst concurrency | 500–3000/min depending on region |
| Environment variables | 4 KB total |
| Payload (sync) | 6 MB; async 256 KB |

## Concurrency types
- **Reserved concurrency**: caps a function and reserves capacity for it. Set to 0 to disable.
- **Provisioned concurrency**: keeps N environments warm. Eliminates cold starts; costs whether invoked or not.

## Async + error handling
- Async invokes get 2 internal retries, then go to a DLQ (SQS/SNS) or a destination (SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge).
- Destinations distinguish success vs failure targets — DLQs are failure-only.

## Cold start mitigation
- Smaller package, less init code, lazy-load SDK clients.
- Use SnapStart (Java, Python, .NET) — snapshots the initialized env.
- Provisioned concurrency tied to an alias for predictable latency.

## Pitfalls
- Long-running Lambdas behind API Gateway — 29s integration limit cuts them off.
- Hot-looping recursive invokes — guard with the recursion-detection feature.

## Related
Step Functions, EventBridge Pipes, App Runner, Fargate.

sheets.title: ECS vs EKS vs Fargate
sheets.summary: ECS for AWS-native simplicity, EKS for standard Kubernetes, Fargate as the serverless launch type for either.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
ECS and EKS are control planes. Fargate is a launch type that removes the EC2 plane underneath either of them.

## Comparison

| Aspect | ECS | EKS | Fargate (on ECS or EKS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| API | AWS-proprietary | Upstream Kubernetes | n/a (launch type) |
| Control plane cost | Free | $0.10/hr per cluster | n/a |
| Data plane | EC2 you manage, or Fargate | EC2/Auto Mode/Karpenter/Fargate | Per-pod/task vCPU + RAM/sec billing |
| Networking | awsvpc, bridge, host | CNI (typically VPC CNI) | awsvpc (ENI per task/pod) |
| Image registry | ECR (private/public) | ECR | ECR |
| IAM | Task role + execution role | IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) / Pod Identity | Same as ECS/EKS |
| Best for | AWS-only shops, simpler ops | Multi-cloud or standard k8s workflows | No node ops, bursty workloads, isolation |

## Key facts
- ECS task definition uses task role (for app calls) vs execution role (to pull images, write logs).
- EKS Pod Identity (newer) is simpler than IRSA and supports multiple clusters per role.
- Fargate has no daemon set, no SSH, no privileged containers, no GPU (yet on all regions).
- ECS Service Connect provides service-mesh-lite for ECS.

## Pitfalls
- Running tiny short-lived jobs on Fargate — the per-task min billing is 1 minute.
- Forgetting EKS control plane is billed per cluster — consolidate non-prod clusters.

## Related
App Runner, Lambda containers, ECR, App Mesh.

sheets.title: CloudFront Origins and Behaviors
sheets.summary: Behaviors map path patterns to origins; OAC secures S3 origins; Functions for tiny edge logic, Lambda@Edge for heavy lifting.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
A distribution has one or more origins (S3, ALB, API Gateway, MediaPackage, or custom HTTP). Behaviors are ordered path-pattern → origin rules with their own caching, headers, and edge functions.

## Origin types

| Origin | Auth method | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| S3 bucket | Origin Access Control (OAC) | OAC replaces legacy OAI; signs SigV4 requests |
| ALB / EC2 / on-prem | Custom HTTP origin | Use signed headers + custom origin keep-alive |
| API Gateway | Custom HTTPS origin | Watch for double-charging cache |
| MediaPackage / S3 (HLS) | OAC + signed URLs | Live and VOD |
| Lambda Function URL | Custom HTTPS | Use OAC for Function URLs (newer feature) |

## Cache behaviors
- Path-pattern matched in order; default behavior catches the rest.
- Per-behavior settings: TTLs, viewer protocol policy, allowed methods, cache key (query strings, headers, cookies), origin request policy, response headers policy.
- Use **Cache Policies** + **Origin Request Policies** instead of legacy forwarded-values.

## Access control
- **Signed URLs**: one URL per file, ideal for single-asset access.
- **Signed cookies**: cover many files at once.
- Both require a trusted key group (CloudFront key pair).

## Edge compute

| Feature | CloudFront Functions | Lambda@Edge |
|---|---|---|
| Runtime | JavaScript (cffjs) | Node.js, Python |
| Max execution | 1 ms CPU | 5s viewer / 30s origin |
| Cold starts | None | Yes |
| Use case | Header rewrites, redirects, A/B | Auth, image manipulation, SSR fragments |

## Pitfalls
- Forgetting to forward the Host header to ALB origins — apps see CloudFront's hostname.
- Using legacy OAI for new buckets — OAC is now the default.

## Related
Global Accelerator, AWS WAF, Route 53, Shield.

sheets.title: Route 53 Routing Policies
sheets.summary: Pick the routing policy that matches the answer you want DNS to give: closest, fastest, healthiest, by-weight, or by-geo.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Route 53 is authoritative DNS with health checks. The routing policy decides which record value gets returned.

## Policies

| Policy | Returns | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| Simple | One record (or random of multiple values) | Single endpoint, no logic |
| Weighted | Proportional split | Canary deploys, A/B by weight |
| Latency | Lowest-latency region from resolver | Multi-region apps optimizing user latency |
| Failover | Primary unless unhealthy, then secondary | Active/passive DR |
| Geolocation | Match continent → country → state | Compliance, content licensing |
| Geoproximity | Bias by geographic distance + adjustable bias | Traffic Flow only; gradual region shift |
| Multi-value answer | Up to 8 healthy records | Client-side load balancing |
| IP-based | Match by client IP CIDR | ISP- or network-specific answers |

## Health checks
- Endpoint, calculated (composite), or CloudWatch alarm.
- Failover and multi-value answer policies require associated health checks to take action.
- Health checks live in `us-east-1` regardless of where the target is.

## Key facts
- Alias records are free and resolve to AWS targets (ALB, CloudFront, S3 website, API Gateway) without extra DNS hops.
- TTLs apply to non-alias records; alias records use the target service's TTL.
- Private hosted zones serve DNS inside one or more VPCs only.
- Route 53 Resolver endpoints connect to on-prem DNS for hybrid lookups.

## Pitfalls
- Mixing routing policies on the same record name without identical types — Route 53 rejects it.
- Long TTLs slowing failover — use 60s or less for records gated by health checks.

## Related
Global Accelerator, CloudFront, AWS Shield, Route 53 Application Recovery Controller (ARC).

sheets.title: CloudFormation vs CDK vs SAM vs Terraform
sheets.summary: CFN is the underlying engine; CDK and SAM compile to CFN; Terraform is its own provider engine with broader reach.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
If you live entirely on AWS and want type-safe code authoring, CDK. If you're shipping serverless apps quickly, SAM. If you also manage Datadog, Cloudflare, and GitHub from the same tool, Terraform.

## Comparison

| Tool | Authoring | State | Drift detection | Multi-cloud |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CloudFormation | YAML/JSON | AWS-managed | Built in (Detect Drift) | No |
| CDK | TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, .NET | Compiles to CFN templates | Via CFN | No (officially) |
| SAM | YAML, transforms to CFN | CFN-managed | Via CFN | No |
| Terraform | HCL | You manage (S3+DDB, Terraform Cloud) | `terraform plan` | Yes (3,000+ providers) |
| Pulumi | Real languages | You manage | Yes | Yes |

## Key facts
- CloudFormation **change sets** preview what will change before you apply.
- **StackSets** deploy a template across many accounts/regions from a delegated admin.
- CDK constructs come in L1 (raw CFN), L2 (sane defaults), and L3 (patterns).
- SAM adds `AWS::Serverless::*` shorthand resources and a local emulator (`sam local invoke`).
- Terraform doesn't natively detect manual changes mid-flight; `terraform plan` reconciles by reading state vs reality at plan time.

## Pitfalls
- Editing a CFN-managed resource in the console — next deploy may replace it.
- Mixing CDK and raw CFN in the same stack without using `addOverride` correctly.
- Storing Terraform state in plain S3 without DynamoDB locking → concurrent applies corrupt state.

## Related
CodeCatalyst, Service Catalog, Config (for compliance), Systems Manager Automation.

sheets.title: CodePipeline Stages and Actions
sheets.summary: Pipelines are ordered stages of parallel actions; each action is source, build, test, deploy, approval, or invoke.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
CodePipeline orchestrates the workflow. CodeBuild compiles, CodeDeploy ships, CodeCommit (legacy) / GitHub / S3 source. Actions inside a stage run in parallel; stages run in order.

## Stage types

| Stage | Typical actions |
|---|---|
| Source | CodeCommit, GitHub (v2 via CodeConnections), Bitbucket, S3, ECR |
| Build | CodeBuild, Jenkins |
| Test | CodeBuild, third-party (e.g., Ghost Inspector) |
| Deploy | CodeDeploy, CloudFormation, ECS, Elastic Beanstalk, S3, OpsWorks, App Runner |
| Approval | Manual approval action (SNS notification) |
| Invoke | Lambda, Step Functions, ECS task |

## Cross-region / cross-account
- Cross-region: artifact bucket per region; replicate or use S3 CRR; specify region in the action.
- Cross-account: use a role in the target account that the pipeline can assume; KMS CMK on the artifact bucket grants encrypt/decrypt to both accounts.

## Key facts
- A pipeline always has at least one source stage. Source change polls or webhook triggers a new execution.
- Manual approval actions can post to SNS for chat/email approval workflows.
- Pipeline executions are now versioned (V2) with named pipeline types — Queued or Parallel.
- Variables can be passed between stages (e.g., output of CodeBuild → input to Lambda invoke).

## Pitfalls
- Pipeline failing on artifact size — default is 5 MB per artifact var, 1 GB per object in artifact bucket; use S3 sources for large bundles.
- Forgetting cross-account KMS permissions — deploys silently fail to decrypt.

## Related
CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CodeArtifact, CodeGuru, CodeCatalyst.

sheets.title: Blue/Green vs Canary vs Rolling
sheets.summary: Blue/green swaps a whole fleet; canary shifts a small slice first; rolling replaces instances in waves.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
Blue/green has the cleanest rollback. Canary catches problems early. Rolling minimizes extra capacity cost. Pick based on tolerance for risk vs cost vs complexity.

## Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Rollback | Cost overhead |
|---|---|---|---|
| In-place / rolling | Replace instances in batches (e.g., 25%) | Reverse the batches | Low — no extra fleet |
| Blue/Green | Stand up parallel "green" environment, switch traffic, retire "blue" | Switch back to blue | High — 2x capacity briefly |
| Canary | Send small % of traffic to new version, then ramp | Stop shifting, route 100% back | Low — small extra |
| Linear | Step-shift X% every N minutes | Same as canary | Low |
| All-at-once | Replace everything immediately | Redeploy old | Risky |

## Service support
- **Lambda** (via aliases + CodeDeploy): Canary10Percent5Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery1Minute, AllAtOnce — supports automatic rollback on alarm.
- **ECS** (CodeDeploy blue/green): swaps target groups behind ALB; supports test traffic on a separate listener.
- **EC2** (CodeDeploy): blue/green or in-place; ELB integration drains connections.
- **App Runner**: managed blue/green.
- **Kubernetes**: Argo Rollouts / Flagger for canary or blue/green patterns.

## Key facts
- CloudWatch alarms can trigger automatic rollback on canary deploys.
- Blue/green DB migrations need backward-compatible schema changes — both versions hit the same DB.
- Feature flags (AppConfig, LaunchDarkly) decouple deploy from release.

## Pitfalls
- Canary without metric alarms is just "slow blast radius" — wire it up.
- Rolling deploys with sticky sessions strand users on draining instances.

## Related
CodeDeploy, AppConfig, Elastic Beanstalk, Step Functions.

sheets.title: KMS Key Types
sheets.summary: AWS-owned (invisible), AWS-managed (per-service, automatic), customer-managed (you control); plus multi-region keys and grants.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
The access control model on KMS is unusual — key policies are the root of trust. IAM alone cannot grant access to a key unless the key policy delegates to IAM.

## Key types

| Type | Who creates | Visible | Policy control | Rotation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AWS-owned | AWS | No | None | AWS-managed |
| AWS-managed (`aws/<service>`) | AWS, per service per account | Yes | Cannot edit policy | Automatic, 1 year |
| Customer-managed (CMK) | You | Yes | Full | Optional, yearly (auto) or on demand |
| Imported key material | You bring BYOK | Yes | Full | Manual reimport |
| Custom key store (CloudHSM-backed or external HSM) | You | Yes | Full | Manual |

## Access mechanisms
- **Key policy**: required, attached to the key. Without an Allow, no principal (not even root) can use the key.
- **IAM policy**: works only if the key policy says "delegate to IAM" (the usual `kms:*` with `Principal: AWS account root`).
- **Grants**: programmatic, fine-grained temporary access — often used by AWS services on your behalf.
- **VPC endpoint policy + key condition keys** (`kms:ViaService`, `aws:SourceVpce`) restrict who and where.

## Multi-region keys
- Same key material replicated to multiple regions, same key ID.
- Useful for DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 cross-region replication of encrypted objects without re-encrypt.

## Key facts
- Automatic rotation generates new backing key material yearly; old material stays available to decrypt existing ciphertexts.
- Asymmetric keys (RSA, ECC) support sign/verify or encrypt/decrypt — never both.
- Key deletion has a 7–30 day waiting period — irreversible after.

## Pitfalls
- Locking yourself out by removing root from key policy with no other Allow.
- Using AWS-managed keys when you need cross-account sharing — you cannot edit those policies.

## Related
Secrets Manager, ACM, CloudHSM, Nitro Enclaves, S3 SSE-KMS.

sheets.title: Well-Architected Framework Pillars
sheets.summary: Six pillars: operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, sustainability.
sheets.body:
## TL;DR
The Well-Architected Framework gives you six lenses to evaluate any workload. The exam loves asking which pillar a given recommendation maps to.

## Pillars

| Pillar | One-line | Example design principle |
|---|---|---|
| Operational Excellence | Run and monitor systems, continuously improve | Make frequent, small, reversible changes; observe everything |
| Security | Protect data, systems, and assets | Apply least privilege, encrypt in transit and at rest, automate response |
| Reliability | Recover from failures, scale to meet demand | Test recovery; design for failure; manage change in automation |
| Performance Efficiency | Use compute resources efficiently and adapt | Go global in minutes; experiment often; use serverless when fit |
| Cost Optimization | Avoid unnecessary spend | Adopt consumption pricing; measure overall efficiency; analyze attribute spend |
| Sustainability | Minimize environmental impact | Right-size; pick efficient regions; avoid idle resources |

## Tooling
- **AWS Well-Architected Tool**: free console workflow; answer questions, get a Milestone, track improvement plans.
- **Lenses**: domain-specific overlays (Serverless, SaaS, ML, IoT, Data Analytics, Financial Services, FTR partner).
- **Trusted Advisor** and **Compute Optimizer** feed concrete recommendations that map back to pillars (mostly Cost + Security + Reliability).

## Key facts
- Sustainability was added in 2021 as the 6th pillar.
- Each pillar has Design Principles, Definitions, and Best Practices — exam questions cite the wording.
- A workload review produces a list of High/Medium/Low risk items per pillar.

## Pitfalls
- Treating WAFR as a one-time audit — it's meant to be revisited at milestones.
- Confusing Operational Excellence (how you run it) with Reliability (whether it stays up).

## Related
AWS Well-Architected Tool, Trusted Advisor, Compute Optimizer, Resilience Hub, Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF).
